title: Can AI Take a Joke—Or Make One? A Study of Humor Generation and Recognition in LLMs
venue: Creativity and Cognition (C&C ’25), June 23–25, 2025, Virtual, United Kingdom
research_questions: How effectively can LLMs generate and interpret humor in emotionally sensitive, support-oriented conversations?
How well do LLMs generate distinct humor styles?
How effectively do LLMs recognize humor in emotional contexts?
How accurately do LLMs identify Counselor versus Friend speaker roles in emotionally sensitive supportive statements?

methods_proposed.name: Dual humor generation and recognition evaluation protocol
methods_proposed.summary: The study evaluates LLM humor generation with shared prompts combining three contextual keywords and an assigned humor style, then uses human raters to assess style alignment, emotional appropriateness, and humor strength. It also evaluates humor recognition by asking LLMs to classify both humor style and speaker role for human-written supportive statements.

humor_theories.detail: The study uses humor styles such as affiliative and self-defeating as generation targets and recognition labels, citing prior work on humor styles.

evaluation_methods.detail: Two human raters independently labeled each LLM-generated statement with the perceived humor type, blind to the original prompt; accuracy was calculated as the percentage of labels matching the intended style.
Human raters rated each generated statement on 5-point Likert scales for appropriateness for emotionally sensitive audiences and humor strength.

For the recognition dataset, each LLM prediction was compared to intended labels for humor style and speaker role, and accuracy was reported as the proportion of correct classifications out of 20 statements.

headline_results.finding: ChatGPT achieved the strongest generated humor style alignment for clear categories, with perfect No Joke alignment and near-perfect Self-Defeating alignment.
headline_results.value: No Joke: μ = 1.00, σ = 0.00; Self-Defeating: μ = 0.93, σ = 0.051

headline_results.finding: Gemini showed moderate generated style alignment for Self-Defeating and No Joke but struggled with Affiliative humor.
headline_results.value: Self-Defeating: μ = 0.71, σ = 0.00; No Joke: μ = 0.60, σ = 0.00; Affiliative: μ = 0.13, σ = 0.129

headline_results.finding: LLaMA3 had lower and more variable generated style alignment on Affiliative humor.
ChatGPT scored highest in emotional appropriateness for generated outputs, especially Affiliative and No Joke responses.
headline_results.value: Affiliative: μ = 4.35, σ = 0.13; No Joke: μ = 4.13, σ = 1.24

headline_results.finding: Affiliative humor was rated the funniest across models, with ChatGPT and LLaMA3 both reaching the reported top mean humor strength.
For recognition, ChatGPT led humor classification while LLaMA3 led role classification, but all reported accuracies were modest.
headline_results.value: Humor classification: ChatGPT μ = 0.65, Gemini 1.5 μ = 0.60, LLaMA3 μ = 0.60; Role classification: LLaMA3 μ = 0.60, ChatGPT μ = 0.55, Gemini 1.5 μ = 0.55
headline_results.best_system: GPT-4o for humor classification; LLaMA3.3 for role classification

key_findings: GPT-4o produced the strongest alignment with intended humor styles, especially for No Joke and Self-Defeating categories, while Affiliative humor remained challenging across models.
GPT-4o also received the highest reported emotional appropriateness ratings, suggesting better tone control in support-oriented contexts.
Affiliative humor was rated the funniest across models, with ChatGPT and LLaMA3 both reported at μ = 3.66 for humor strength.
Humor and role recognition were only modestly accurate: ChatGPT performed best on humor classification, while LLaMA3 performed best on speaker role identification.
The authors argue that current LLM humor is often fluent but derivative or formulaic, and that human oversight is important in emotionally sensitive applications.
limitations: The dataset size is relatively small and constrained in scope.
The small, homogeneous rater pool may introduce subjective bias and reduce annotation reliability.
The study uses static, single-turn responses rather than fluid, evolving real conversations.
The authors state that future work should include a broader variety of humor types, emotional tones, and interaction scenarios, as well as more numerous and diverse raters.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper emphasizes human oversight for humor-sensitive applications such as counseling, peer support, mental health, and educational tutoring, because misaligned humor can undermine rapport, emotional safety, and trust.

human_samples.population: human raters; the paper later characterizes the rater pool as small and homogeneous but gives no demographics
human_samples.observations: 60 AI-generated statements; each statement was independently labeled for perceived humor type and rated on 5-point appropriateness and humor-strength scales
human_samples.detail: For the Humor Generation Dataset, two human raters independently labeled each LLM-generated statement with the perceived humor type, blind to the original prompt. The same rater pool rated emotional appropriateness and humor strength.

human_samples.population: researcher crafting study materials; not further described
human_samples.observations: 20 human-authored supportive statements with intended speaker-role and humor-condition labels
human_samples.detail: The Humor Recognition Dataset contains 20 responses crafted by a researcher to simulate emotionally sensitive scenarios, varying by speaker role (Counselor or Friend) and humor condition (Affiliative, Self-Defeating, or No Joke).

judges.detail: Two human raters independently classified each generated statement for perceived humor type; they were blind to the original prompt. Style-alignment accuracy was calculated from whether these labels matched the intended style.

Human raters rated generated statements on 5-point Likert scales for appropriateness in emotionally neutral/sensitive situations and humor strength. The paper does not state whether raters were blind to model identity or other metadata for these ratings.
For the recognition task, model predictions were compared to intended labels for humor style and speaker role attached to the 20 researcher-crafted human-written statements.

judges.name: accuracy / average Likert aggregation
judges.detail: The paper computed proportions/percentages correct for style alignment, humor classification, and role classification, and averaged Likert ratings by LLM and humor type.

generation_selection.detail: The paper reports 60 final AI-generated statements, 20 from each LLM, produced from shared prompts combining three keywords and an assigned humor style. It does not report whether each final statement came from a single raw generation, whether outputs were regenerated or filtered, or whether any best-of-N selection was used.

model_provenance.name: ChatGPT
model_provenance.detail: Also described as ChatGPT-4o/GPT-4o. No API endpoint, snapshot, inference date, or access method is reported.

model_provenance.name: Gemini
model_provenance.detail: Reported as Gemini 1.5. No exact endpoint/snapshot, inference date, or access method is reported.

model_provenance.name: Meta LLaMA
model_provenance.detail: Also referred to as LLAMA3/Llama3 in parts of the paper. No model size, checkpoint, deployment method, or inference date is reported.

inference_budgets.detail: The prompt template is provided: generate a sentence in a specified humor style incorporating three keywords and suitable for general/emotionally sensitive situations. The paper reports 20 final generated statements per model but not number of calls, retries, candidate counts, token limits, decoding parameters, compute, or cost.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: One described pass per statement-model pair; 20 statements were passed to each of ChatGPT, Llama, and Gemini, with no repeated calls reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The prompt asked each LLM to choose a humor style from No Joke, Self-Defeating, and Affiliative, choose Counselor or Friend, and briefly explain the choice. Token limits, decoding settings, output parsing details, compute, and cost are not reported.

human_baselines.description: No human-authored responses to the same keyword/style generation prompts were evaluated as a baseline. Human raters evaluated only LLM-generated statements.
human_baselines.detail: The study compares generated outputs across ChatGPT/GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5, and LLaMA3.3, not against matched human-generated humor.

human_baselines.description: The recognition items were human-authored and used as test stimuli/ground truth, but the paper reports no human classifier performance on the same 20 statements.
human_baselines.detail: LLM predictions were compared to intended labels; no directly comparable human recognition baseline is provided.

mitigations: The paper describes both evaluation datasets as developed for this study rather than drawn from a public legacy benchmark.
The recognition statements were crafted by a researcher for the study.
contamination_risk.basis: The main evaluated items are newly introduced study-specific prompts/statements, and the supplied paper gives no indication that the benchmark datasets were public before the evaluated model versions. This lowers benchmark-memorization risk. Residual uncertainty remains because model inference dates and decontamination checks are not reported, and at least some joke content may resemble common public jokes or templates.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Generated-output conclusions rely on two human raters for style labels and Likert ratings; recognition conclusions compare evaluated LLM predictions to researcher-intended labels.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: The evaluated LLMs perform generation and classification tasks, but the paper does not use a separate LLM as an evaluator, metric, or judge of another model’s outputs.

reporting_gaps: Rater demographics, recruitment, geography, expertise, and language background are not reported; the paper only notes a small, homogeneous rater pool.
Inter-rater agreement, adjudication procedures, and the exact aggregation method for the two raters’ labels/ratings are not fully reported.
Model access method, exact API/provider endpoint or checkpoint, inference dates, and LLaMA3.3 deployment/model-size details are not reported.
Generation-time inference settings are not reported, including temperature, top-p, seed, max tokens, number of calls, retries, candidate counts, filtering, or best-of-N selection.
Recognition-time inference settings are not reported, including decoding parameters, retries, token limits, and how model explanations/outputs were parsed when computing accuracy.
The full prompt/keyword set, full generated dataset, full recognition statements, and complete label distributions are not provided in the supplied paper text; only samples are shown.

The humor-style inventory is underspecified or inconsistent: Methods/examples mention styles such as aggressive, wordplay/puns, and dry humor, while the reported evaluation categories/results focus on Affiliative, Self-Defeating, and No Joke.
The researcher-created intended labels for the recognition dataset are not reported as independently validated by human annotators.
No matched human performance baseline is reported for either generation or recognition.
Compute and monetary cost are not reported.
